items.text: I don't have prejudice against meself. My father was a white and my mother was black. Them call me half-caste or whatever. Me don't dip on nobody's side. Me don't dip on the black man's side nor the white man's side. Me dip on God's side, the one who create me and cause me to come from black and white.
items.context: Discussing his mixed racial identity and philosophical approach to race and identity in the context of Rastafarian belief and spirituality
items.publicReaction: The statement became widely circulated as expressing Marley's perspective on transcending racial divisions and being guided by spiritual principle rather than racial identity politics
items.date: 2012 (as featured in documentary, statement from earlier period of his life)
items.source: Marley (2012) documentary directed by Kevin Macdonald

items.text: What is your special message? Truth, peace and love and music, you know. And livity.
items.context: Interview during the Kaya tour in Norway in 1978, when asked about his special message
items.publicReaction: The quote was widely shared across Bob Marley's official social media accounts (X, Facebook, TikTok) and represents a concise statement of Marley's core philosophy during the Kaya era
items.source: Official Bob Marley social media accounts; video interview from Norway Kaya tour

items.text: The people who making this world worse aren't taking the day off.
items.context: Response when asked why he decided to perform at Smile Jamaica concert despite being shot two days earlier (December 3, 1976)
items.publicReaction: Widely cited as demonstrating Marley's commitment to his principles and message despite personal danger; the quote became iconic of his determination
items.source: Legend Reggae documentation and historical records of Smile Jamaica concert

items.text: I'm not singing 'Death to the oppressed. Everyone was born to live.
items.context: Interview featured in the Reggae Sunsplash 1979 documentary, discussing the message of his music
items.publicReaction: Represented Marley's philosophy of life affirmation and positive messaging rather than violent rebellion
items.source: Reggae Sunsplash II (1979) documentary film directed by Stefan Paul

items.text: We try fe mek really bring peace, knowing that we really can't solve a problem with a war, y'know.
items.context: Interview in Toronto during the Kaya tour in June 1978 (June 9, prior to Maple Leaf Gardens performance), conducted by reporter Sandie Rinaldo
items.publicReaction: Shared across official Bob Marley social media platforms and YouTube, reflecting his pacifist philosophy during the Kaya era
items.source: Official Bob Marley social media accounts; video interview from Toronto Kaya tour

items.text: I just want to shake hands and show the people that we're gonna make it right, we're gonna unite, we're gonna make it right, we've got to unite.
items.context: Statement made on stage at the One Love Peace Concert while joining hands with Jamaican Prime Minister Michael Manley and Opposition Leader Edward Seaga during the performance
items.publicReaction: The moment became iconic in Jamaican and reggae history, widely documented and celebrated as a gesture for national unity and peace during a period of violent political conflict
items.source: Biographica historical records and Jamaica Gleaner archives documenting the One Love Peace Concert

items.text: Just let me tell you something (yeah), to make ever... and through the spirit of the Most High, His Imperial Majesty Emperor Haile Selassie I, we're inviting a few leading people of the slaves to shake hands…To show the people that you love them right, to show the people that you gonna unite, show the people that you're over bright, show the people that everything is all right.
items.context: Extended remarks at the One Love Peace Concert stage while calling Michael Manley and Edward Seaga onstage and holding their hands above his head
items.publicReaction: Widely documented as a pivotal moment in Jamaican history; the speech demonstrated Marley's integration of Rastafarian spirituality with political activism
items.source: Biography.com historical documentation of the One Love Peace Concert

items.text: The world will be watching, since them gonna follow the example we set. When we come together the whole world gonna look and seh, 'It can be done.'
items.context: Interview at his home the day before the One Love Peace Concert, speaking to reporters about the significance of the unity gesture
items.publicReaction: Cited as demonstrating Marley's strategic understanding of the concert's global symbolic importance
items.source: HISTORY magazine documentation of the One Love Peace Concert

items.text: He's a hard worker, but he didn't know my music. I don't want to put him down, but reggae isn't really his bag.
items.context: Comment about Johnny Nash's recording and commercial success with 'Stir It Up,' which Marley had originally written and recorded
items.publicReaction: Documented as Marley's assessment of Nash's lack of deep understanding of reggae music, representing his perspective on cultural authenticity

items.text: On your way up, take me up. On your way down, don't let me down. Money can't buy life.
items.context: Final words spoken to his son Ziggy Marley while hospitalized in Miami, close to death from cancer
items.publicReaction: Widely cited as Marley's final testament and philosophical statement about life values and family loyalty
items.source: TODAY Show and Ziggy Marley interviews about his father's death; documented in biographical materials about Bob Marley's final days

items.text: Leave you, go where? What are you crying for? Forget crying, Rita! Just keep singing. Sing! Sing!
items.context: Response to Rita Marley at his hospital bed in Miami as she broke down crying, urging her to continue singing instead of crying
items.publicReaction: Cited as demonstrating Marley's commitment to music and spirituality even in the face of death; reflects his character and priorities
items.source: Rita Marley's book 'No Woman, No Cry: My Life with Bob Marley' and biographical documentation of his final days

items.text: Emancipate yourself from mental slavery. None but ourselves can free our minds.
items.context: Opening lyrics of 'Redemption Song,' the final track on his 1980 album Uprising; based on a 1937 speech by Marcus Garvey at Menelik Hall in Sydney, Nova Scotia
items.publicReaction: Became one of Marley's most iconic and quoted statements; represents the philosophical core of his spiritual and political message; widely adopted as a mantra for liberation movements
items.source: Bob Marley and the Wailers album Uprising; originally from Marcus Garvey's 1937 speech published in Black Man magazine

items.context: Repeated exclamation during his performance at Zimbabwe's independence celebration concert at Rufaro Stadium in Harare, calling out between songs
items.publicReaction: Widely documented as the only outside performer invited to Zimbabwe's independence ceremony; his appearance and statements were celebrated as a symbolic gesture of Pan-African solidarity
items.source: Multiple historical sources including Al Jazeera, Forbes, and Africa.com documentation of Zimbabwe independence celebrations
